content.full_text:
EDMUND G, BROWN JR. 
GOVERNOR 
September 29, 1976 
GOVERNOR'S OFFICE 
SACRAMENTO 95814 
The Honorable Pauline Davis 
Assemblywoman 
Room 4148 
State Capitol 
Sacramento, California 
Dear Assemblywoman Davis: 
This is in response to your inquiry concerning possible 
conflicts of interest of meniliers of the California Horse 
Racing Board. 
Mr. Nathaniel Colley, the Governor's recent appointee to 
the Board, holds no stock or other interest in a California 
race track licensed by the Board. 
I understand that neither 
of the other two members of the Board holds any interest in 
a California race track. 
Thus, to the best of my knowledge, 
the disqualification provisions of Business and Professions 
Code Section 19423 are inapplicable to the current members 
of the board. 
There is no statutory prohibition against a Board member 
owning a breeding farm, or stable of race horses. 
Business and Professions Code Section 19424 explicitly permits 
a Board member to receive a purse as an owner or breeder of 
California horses. 
It is thus the Legislature's apparent intent 
to permit horse owners and breeders to sit as meniliers of the 
California Horse Racing Board. 
The Political Reform Act of 1974 does not prohibit a member of 
the Horse Racing Board from holding an interest in a breeding 
farm. 
It is conceivable that a situation may arise in which a 
Board member's action will have a reasonably forseeable material 
financial effect on his breeding farm alone. 
Whether such a 
conflict exists, and whether the financial effect is "material" 
or "reasonably forseeable" are questions of fact that must be 
determined on a case by case basis. 
In the event that such a 
conflict does arise, a Board menilier would be required to 
disqualify himself from acting on a matter that would have a 
forseeable material financial 
fect on his breeding farm. 


Hon. Pauline Davis 
(2) 
September 29, 1976 
However, I do not believe that conflict situations would 
arise with sufficient frequency to inhibit a horse breeder's 
ability to function as an effective member of the Board. 
The proposed Conflict of Interest Code for the California 
Horse Racing Board will soon be under review by the Fair 
Political Practices Commission. 
As the questions that you 
raise will be dealt with in detail by this Code, I suggest 
that you direct further inquiries to that Commission. 
Sincerely, 
Peter W. Sly 
Assistant Legal Affairs 
Secretary

Carlotta Mellon

sections.question_synthetic: Do members of the California Horse Racing Board have conflicts of interest if they own breeding farms or race horses, and are there statutory prohibitions on such ownership under the Political Reform Act of 1974 and Business and Professions Code sections 19423-19424?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The Governor's office concludes that no statutory prohibition exists against Board members owning breeding farms or race horses. While case-by-case conflicts of interest may arise requiring disqualification on specific matters, such conflicts would not arise with sufficient frequency to impede a horse breeder's ability to serve effectively on the Board. The FPPC's Conflict of Interest Code review will address these issues further.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document contains two separate letters: (1) incoming request from Davis to Governor's office dated September 24, 1976, and (2) Governor's response dated September 29, 1976. No formal FPPC advice letter format detected. The response addresses the substantive legal question but is from Governor's office, not FPPC. Marked as 'correspondence' rather than official FPPC advice letter due to the originating source and informal structure. Minor OCR errors present ('meniliers' instead of 'members', 'fct' instead of 'effect').

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Do members of the California Horse Racing Board have conflicts of interest if they own breeding farms or race horses, and are there statutory prohibitions on such ownership under the Political Reform Act of 1974 and Business and Professions Code sections 19423-19424?

CONCLUSION: The Governor's office concludes that no statutory prohibition exists against Board members owning breeding farms or race horses. While case-by-case conflicts of interest may arise requiring disqualification on specific matters, such conflicts would not arise with sufficient frequency to impede a horse breeder's ability to serve effectively on the Board. The FPPC's Conflict of Interest Code review will address these issues further.

embedding.summary: This is correspondence between Assemblywoman Pauline Davis and the Governor's Office regarding potential conflicts of interest for California Horse Racing Board members who own breeding farms. The Governor's office provides a legal opinion on applicable statutes and the Political Reform Act of 1974.
